sentences.text: My name is Rudolph, written R-U-D-O-L-P-H Roden, R-O-D-E-N.
The middle name is George, but I rarely use.
At birth, my name was R-O-S-E-N, Rosen.
Was Rudalf, only written as an F at the end instead of the PH.
Ah, middle name was J-I-R-- with the hooklet on it-- and -I, pronounced 'year-zhi', unpronounceable.
Yes.
My Hebrew name was [?
Abraham ?] [?
Mayer. ?]
Most people call me Ruda.
It would be written R-U-D-A.
Or, when I came here, it was Rudy, either R-U-D-I or R-U-D-Y.
I was born on the 27th of August, 1923 in Prague, 'Pra-ha.'
In Czechoslovakia, at that point in time.
You know, it is interesting.
When I am going to Prague now, or when we went to Prague last few times, I only appreciate what Prague is.
When you are little, so-- you don't realize where you actually live.
You live there.
The house I was born became a part of what the Russians called the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the integral, when they take a throughway and lead it through a city.
So it was part, simply one of those stream of this throughway is the street I was born.
I was born at home.
And I remember my birth very well, for it was repeated to me by my mother on numerous occasions with all the details and all the difficulties.
The address was 39.
The street was called Leger-- what would one pronounce it here-- 'le-jay', L-E-G-E-R-- 'Leg-ay-rova' as all the female gender in Czech and Slovakian and Polish pronounce it O-V-A.
Yes.
Let's say I will dismiss the throughway.
And it was a quiet street about six stories-- five, six stories high.
We lived on the third floor.
And we occupied half of a-- of the floor.
My memory is a little holey.
It's caused by a fact, which I mention in a little while.
I can remember under the age of six only a certain kind of vignettes.
And to take you down the street, if I would walk up from our house about four blocks, there was a square called the Square of Peace.
And I was going to the grammar school there in the Square of Peace.
My mother was an extremely nice human being, a little pushy Jewish mother, who simply decided that I am born in such a year that I would have to wait for the school another year.
So I went to school when I was five, which didn't bother me so much.
Yes.
Obecna Skola na Mirovem Namesti.
Now, I will spell it, I got myself into it-- O-B-E-C-N-A, with a line on it.
The next one is S-- with a hooklet-- K-O-L-A, na, N-A, capital M, M-I-R-O-V-E-M Namesti, N-A-M-E-- there's an E with a hooklet S-T-I.
I will avoid next time.
[LAUGHS] It's still called very similarly now, in spite of all the change of regimes and return to the democracy again.
And now, from the grade four, one could do a test into something, which would be called a high school here.
We called it the gymnasium, gymnasium, which didn't mean anything when you do I mean your exercises and you train sport.
But it was a similar type of school, which the French call a lycee.
It was an eight-years proposition.
And one could pass a special test from grade four instead of grade five.
And if you passed the test, you were permitted to go there.
So naturally, who didn't pass the test?
So I appeared to the gymnasium, being approximately two years younger than the rest of the class.
I was also a one and only Jew in the class.
And I never felt any pressure, any kind of speck of anti-Semitism.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, we were sitting three in a bench, I was sitting in the last bench.

Next to me was a friend of mine who just died this spring-- on whose account I went to Prague about two weeks ago-- who actually was with me in the same class since grade one of the grammar school in the primary grade.
On the other side was, again, a friend of mine who died about 10 years ago.
We all became doctors.
The friend of mine on my right side became a quite famous neurosurgeon.
And the other one was an internist, who got himself killed in a car accident.
They both were supposedly Roman Catholics, but never really practiced anything, same as I didn't.
Ah, then--
I entered the gymnasium in 1932.
And it was funnily enough the-- we moved from my place of birth really very far, about one and quarter of a block.
For my father bought there an apartment, which was a little bit bigger.
And the building was a little bit more modern.
It had an elevator.
And the gymnasium was literally two blocks from there in the street called Londynska, which means London street.
And so I somehow got accustomed that always the place of duty and the place of work should be close.
I would have preceded my studies, but in March 15, 1939, on the Ides of March, the-- Hitler's army marched in and occupied Czechoslovakia.
I don't want to go to the political aspect of it.
I was standing on the famous Wenzel's Square, W-E-N-Z-E-L apostrophe S square, and cried.
We all did.
And people really just looked stunned.

I discovered when I returned to my classes that two of my professors-- we call them professors-- the guy who was teaching German, his name was Janecek, J-A-N-E-C-E-K, he came to the class and had the swastika button in his lapel.
And about two weeks later, the professor who was teaching Greek-- his name was Bozka, B-O-Z-- with a hooklet K-A-- came in a SI uniform.
And there was one Jewish student.
It became practically untenable to do anything, so that I didn't prepare myself for German or for Greek at all.
It had no sense.
I would be torn apart.
And I would be asked questions which had nothing to do with the subject whatsoever.
My German was good, probably as good as the professor's.
But I pretended that I stopped, I forgot.
I saw that at the end, when there was finally the matric being written, I was left to write something which was not a matric, for I couldn't write anything in-- in Greek or in German.
And I was given a [INAUDIBLE] kind of pass.
For at that point, I obtained visa, student's visa, to the United States.
And I could have continued at that point at the [INAUDIBLE] High School in Bronx.
For my aunt, who was a sister of my mother's, by the name of Rose, Rose Caplan written as a C, C-A-P-L-A-N-- sent me this paper.
Said she wanted to send the papers for the whole family, which caused the affidavit.
And I could have left, but I couldn't do it on account of my parents For something happened when I was 11 years old.
So my four years older sister Hilda, H-I-L-D-A, came one day late from [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a visit to our grandfather who lived-- from maternal grandfather-- who lived in the country.
She went there with a friend who are our neighbors.
And they were supposed to return Saturday night with the last train, and they didn't.
They came home Sunday morning train.
And a lady came from the place where they spend the evening dancing.
She was 15 years old.
And it was reported that they stayed in a hotel.
Most probably they stayed with some men, and most probably something drastic must have happened.
And a calamity broke out in our family.
I have never seen my parents simply so angry, and so abusive, and so loud.
And the only thing which explained it was the morals of the times.
And Hilda was beaten and was yelled upon and screamed.
And they wanted to know the truth, whether she's still a virgin or not or something similar.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was sent to bed.
And I was thinking, what would I do if I would be Hilda.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I decided, I probably would jump out the window.
We lived on the sixth floor.
And that would save me from any kind of disgrace.
And I was woken up at 5:00 in the morning with a thud.
And I jumped out of bed, of my bed.
And I opened the door to Hilda's bedroom.
And I have seen the window open.
I have seen the curtains somehow flying.
And I just heard the drops of the rain hitting the windowsill.
And I knew what happened.
She jumped.
That changed my life, for my parents were absolutely desolate.
And they thought that they killed their daughter for the autopsy was found out that nothing happened.
And so did I, I think, that they were murderers of my sister.
Took me quite a while to come to the realization that life is something quite different.
And something happened to my life.
I was never forbidden anything.
I was never given any orders.
I was never corrected.
I could do absolutely anything what I wanted.
Luckily, the professors at the gymnasium, without me realizing at that point in time, they somehow were leading me a little bit.
Especially the guy who was our headmaster, who taught Czech, was a remarkable person.
And somehow, when I wanted to take a trip on a canoe or a kayak, somewhere I could do it.
I just got the money.

When I wanted to do this kind of sport or that kind of sport or to go, I mean, to a summer camp or not, or to go to my grandfather whom I loved, my grandmother-- they lived in the country-- I could do that.
Maybe it hurt me later on.
For I was pretty self-dependent, became pretty self-dependent.
Hm?
You know, I went through six years of psychoanalysis.
In spite of that, I have just very, very sporadic memory for the trauma probably.
And especially, the indictment of the parents was so big that it simply blocked the memories.
I say, I know just the little holes.
I see Hilda.
We are having a lunch, or a dinner.
And she is giving me some kind of food I like.
I don't know whether it was a wiener or whether it was a piece of salami or something like it.
And she said her-- one of her usuals, which was, take it, just take it, I can't anymore.
Or, take it, I don't like it.
Also, I remember we are out.
I am trying to get cherries from a cherry tree at my grandfather's.
And she's using a little hoe to hit the cherries, so that they would fall down.
And she hit me-- hits me in the forehead.
And I am being taken to a doctor or to a hospital, I don't know exactly.
I remember Hilda when she was going to some kind of a dance.
And she's prancing in front of me in her underwear.
At that point, I am nine years old, so it doesn't mean too much.
And she's putting all kinds of stuff on her.
It's about all what I remember.
My father was a very kind man who worked as a head of the sales and head of the office of a big company, which was mining and selling coal.
The name was Polak, P-O-L-A-K.
His name was Berthold, B-E-R-T-H-O-L-D.
But he was called mostly Bert only, B-E-R-T.
His family name was Rosen, R-O-S-E-N.
He was born on the 13th of May, 19-- sorry, 1886 in Warsaw.
And the family moved from Warsaw to Prague when he was about 4 years old.
My paternal grandfather, who at that point in time lived in Warsaw, was born in Odessa in Russia.
And he was one of the exceptions, where the Tsar permitted a kind of rich or prominent Jew to live in Odessa.
My grandfather's name on the paternal side was Abraham.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On my maternal side his name was Josef.
Spitz, S-P-I-T-Z.
This year, I wanted to find his grave.
For he died in 1940, when we couldn't go back and forth to visit him anymore for there was a curfew for Jews.
And he was buried in the place where I knew my great-grandfather was born, very close to the place where he lived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The place is a village, which we visited about three weeks ago.
Again, I have to spell the name, K-R-A-S-O-N-O-V-I-C-E, Krasonovice.

And he was buried in something which is called-- where was a small Jewish cemetery in Z-B-R-A-S-L-A-V-I-C-E, Zbraslavice where there was also a small temple shul, which was destroyed, which became, I think, a school.
And the small cemetery in Zbraslavice, I couldn't find.
This year, I found it.
I found also that the Czechs were not so bad.
They left the tombstone standing.
And somebody took care of the grass.
Only, somehow, the wall disappeared.
There are, more or less, summer cottages around it.
For there is-- it's on a hill.
And underneath, there's a brook or a little river.
And I found there are 16 Spitz's 16 graves, tombs of various Spitz's.
It must have been a kind of endemic name in that region.
I-- with the years, I taught myself that I was closer to my maternal grandfather.
But I think it just caused by the fact that, at that point, I didn't forgive my father for my sister.
I'm sure I was closer to my father.
Once Hitler marched in, so the company was dissolved practically within a few months.
And he was quite a resourceful man, but he became pretty desolate and defeated.
For [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the whole family, family life, and family clan, which was around was disrupted by this.
And it was obvious that he should have left.
And I think he blamed himself for the fact that he obeyed my mother, and we didn't leave.
For my mother wanted to stay, for her parents were alive.
And everybody around she knew was there.
So why should she leave?
So there was actually discussion in the family about the possibility of leaving?
Yes.
It was many times.
And I was quite adamant that we should leave, that this will lead to something drastic, something terrible like is happening in Germany.
But my parents didn't heed.
They could imagine-- couldn't imagine the extent of the repercussions which will follow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my father, naturally, in Terezin where he was working something, sorting the-- clothing, which probably was sent then to Germany.
And then I remember him in Auschwitz in [INAUDIBLE] where he didn't do anything significant.
I was in the position then to shield him that he didn't have to do anything.
My didn't-- my father did not pass the final selection, so that I have seen him the last time when I left.
It was on the 1st of July, 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was gassed in July 6, 1944.
My mother was born in that village I named, Krasonovice.
She was born on the 23-- 22nd of December, 19-- sorry, 1892.
She had three siblings, two brothers and one sister.
She was, I think, the oldest.
Her sister Rose was the youngest.
She moved to the United States when she was 16 years old.
My older uncle, his name was Bedrich, B-E-D-R-I-C-H.
You could say 'Free-drich' in German.
I liked him very much.
I will say something more about him.
And then I had a brother-- then I had an uncle who died in Russia during the First World War being in the Czech army.
His name was Rudolph.
I was named after him.
In fact, I was named originally Walter, W-A-L-T-E-R.
But my mother realized that she made a mistake.
And when I was one and a half years old, through the order of parliament, my name was changed to Rudolph.
My mother's name was Franzisca, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] written F-R-A-N-Z-I-S-C-A.
And she was a very motherly type.
Her maiden name was Spitz, S-P-I-T-Z.
Hannah, I think, was her name, her Jewish name, which I just found out, which was not used.
She was a very kind person, a very kind of determined person.
When she made up her mind, it was very difficult to change it.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she went through Terezin and through Auschwitz.
She passed the selection.
And, interestingly enough, after the war, I waited whether she would return.
And then, at the beginning of July, I see myself typing a letter to my aunt in-- who lived at that point in New York-- describing a little bit our fate, what happened about till that point.
I said that my mother supposedly left Auschwitz.

But as she did not report or there are any-- and there are no news about her up until this point in time, I have to take for granted that she-- and I didn't write the word died, for somebody rang the bell.
I opened the door, it was mother.
Fine.
Perfect, go ahead.
